narrative:
Scattered showers are possible much of today. The more widespread rain misses south this morning, and we are left with hit-or-miss opportunities. Your best chance of a dry window for outdoor activity will be midday through late afternoon, after which a round of scattered showers and thundershowers develop along the incoming cold front. We are left with drier, cooler conditions Thursday and Friday before a chilly light rain invades on Saturday.

It's a week of wetter weather with a cooler start to May. This week's Monday Message is now available to subscribers.

2027 Blue Ridge Calendar Photo Contest

Voting is open for the 2027 photo contest.

We received 854 photos from 211 photographers in the 2027 Blue Ridge Calendar Photo Contest. From those, our five judges chose 20 finalists from each season from 53 photographers. You can vote every day. The voting period closes May 3.

Get the free RWC Mobile App...: Apple and Android

The more widespread rain misses south this morning, and we are left with hit-or-miss opportunities. Your best chance of a dry window for outdoor activity will be midday through late afternoon, after which a round of scattered showers and thundershowers develop along the incoming cold front. We are left with drier, cooler conditions Thursday and Friday before a chilly light rain invades on Saturday. It's a week of wetter weather with a cooler start to May. Subscribe Tuesday Extended forecast is only available for RaysWeather.com supporters. Subscribe Forecast Discussion Scattered showers will be possible throughout the day, although they will primarily be focused on the morning hours and the late afternoon & evening hours, with a window of less activity from midday until late afternoon. A few thundershowers will be possible in the late day window. All of this activity is helped by an incoming cold front that marks the front end of a change to much cooler weather for several days. Gone (for now) are the low 80s in Valle Crucis and Galax: we are talking afternoon temperatures in the 50s and 60s for most of the forecast period. The front clears the area this evening and brings breezy, dry conditions through Thursday afternoon. Temperatures stay cool on Friday with a rather chilly morning... valleys are into the 30s when you wake up. The next weather-maker arrives early Saturday morning in the form of a low pressure system gliding northeastward from the Gulf, as if it is wintertime. Expect light rain to settle in sometime Saturday morning, and last into early afternoon at least. Fog and low clouds will keep things socked in much of the day, if not all day. It won't be out of the question to get a couple mountain-top flurries late Saturday evening either. Sunday is still quite cool after a cold morning near or below freezing, depending on your elevation. Sunday and Monday mornings bring the greatest threat of frost and freeze conditions. Moderation begins on Monday as scattered shower chances may return overnight into Tuesday amidst a spring rebound. Detailed forecast discussion and analysis is only available to RaysWeather supporters. Subscribe Log in Jack Kendrick Meteorologist PHOTO OF THE DAY CLICK TO ENLARGE Spring-O-Meter- Nice Dusting of Fringed Phacelia! SUbmitted by Scott J. All Photos Submit Photo Latest Insights Breaking Down The Drought One of the most (if not the most) prevalent topics of the last month on RaysWeather.Com has been the ongoing drought that is exhibiting a tight grip on our region. With how much we have been mentioning it in our forecast discussions and videos, we figured it is about time that we do a deeper dive into the impacts and the numbers associated with the drought as we wrap up April. How Dry Is It? Well, pretty darn dry. The Hickory airport experienced it's driest six month period from October 25th to April 25th in the 77-year history at the site, with just 12.32 inches of rain. That's over 10 inches less than is normal for that period of time! Over that same six month period, it has been: 4th driest in Boone Driest on record at Beech Mountain Driest on record in Celo 2nd driest in Hot Springs Driest on record in Galax Driest on record in Old Fort Driest on record in Brevard 8th driest in Asheville 15th driest in Wytheville Now, this is for a somewhat arbitrary period of time, but it illustrates the point well. Many locations locally experienced their combined driest end of fall, winter, and start of spring on record. The spring season to date has been exceptionally warm and dry as well. Through that same date of April 25th, 9 local reporting sites have logged their warmest spring on record (so far): Boone, Beech Mountain, Hickory, Asheville, Old Fort, Celo, Hot Springs, Galax, and Mountain City, TN. Mean temperatures are running anywhere from 5 to 8 degrees above normal, and are being boosted greatly by how warm our afternoons have been. Afternoon high temperatures are running 8 to 10 degrees above normal, while morning lows sit 4 to 6 degrees above normal. So, What Does That Mean? For many of us that are not farmers, it can be a little harder to see what the impacts of a drought are beyond the lowered lakes and rivers around here. Most of the region is outlined in the level 3 "extreme drought," which is the lighter red coloring on the cover image, and has been for multiple weeks. This is indeed manifesting in more than just lakes running below full pool: the towns of Morganton and North Wilkesboro have sought voluntary water conservation from citizens. No enforcement of any restrictions, but being proactive in the event the drought persists and/or worsens. Caldwell County has put in place minor water restrictions, and local farmers have reported that they are having to cut crops. If the drought should persist, harvests later this year will not be what they usually are, and further water restrictions may be necessary for counties and municipalities. Burn bans in North Carolina and Virginia may be extended further into the end of spring and start of summer, and wildfire danger will stay elevated. The National Park Service is beginning a project this summer to remove Helene debris from 3,000 acres of Blue Ridge Parkway-adjacent land in order to mitigate the wildfire risk, as the volume of downed trees and limbs can serve as a timber box for any fire that sparks. Will There Ever Be Any Relief? As we wrap up the month of April and creep closer to summer in the southeast, the "method" for rain begins to shift. Over the fall, winter, and most of spring, most of our rain comes from individual low pressure systems that cut through the region and provide long-lasting, soaking rains. As the calendar flips to May, the transition to summertime pop-up thunderstorms is in full swing, bringing plenty of days where some folks recieve an inch or two of rain while others are left high and dry. We have recieved two beneficial rainfall events in a three-day stretch as of this writing, one on Saturday 4/25 and one on Tuesday 4/28. Several stations across the mountains recorded over an inch of rain from these two events combined. That is helpful, but it is far from a drought-ending event. In fact, with a drought this prolonged, there is almost certainly no one event that would end the drought. Many areas are 8 to 12 inches behind schedule over the past six months, and it will take several soaking rains to catch up to where we should be heading into the summer. Check out Rosman, down in the bottom left corner in the wettest area in the Eastern US: almost 20 inches below normal for the last six months! It will likely be difficult to get rid of the drought over the course of the summer, simply from the nature of summertime precipitation being generally more scattered. An 8 to 12 inch deficit is not one that is quickly erased. It will take months of average to above average precipitation to clear out that deficit, but the short-term goal is simply to get out of the more severe drought that we are currently in, which can be accomplished over a shorter period of time with some cooperation from the weather. There is reason for optimism, with a more active pattern finally settling in to end April and begin May after weeks of dry sunshine, as well as burgeoning El Nino conditions developing through fall and winter, which tends to lead to more opportunities for precipitation. Even so, at least some level of drought is likely to continue for several months. Apr 28, 2026 Monday Message for April 27, 2026 Multiple rain opportunities are on the table as we enter May with an unseasonably cool start to the month. Check out all of the banter on this week's Monday Message. Apr 27, 2026 February 2026 Recap Monthly Recap Not-So-Frigid February Unlike January, which followed a more “trackable” pattern of starting mild and ending cold, February was a little more helter-skelter, with a cold and snowy start, followed by an interrupted, generally mild pattern over weeks two through four. Not to say that February was a mild month, that would be a mischaracterization. Most of the final numbers ended up near to slightly above (think 2 or 3 degrees) average. The statewide snowstorm that wrapped up January bled into the 1st of February for those in the mountains, and a largely chilly first 10 days of the month delivered two more snow events, with a light event in the Foothills on the 5th and a light/moderate northwest flow event on the 6th & 7th. The first 10 days of the month all had below freezing lows in Hickory, which totaled 18 consecutive freezes when going back into late January. Nothing out of the ordinary, but a good sign of long-lasting cold. On the 10th itself, a quietly remarkable occurrence unfolded in the Foothills, as morning lows in the mid to upper 20s preceded afternoon highs in the low 70s. Temperature swings of above 40 degrees are a rarity around here, and Edgemont’s 46 degree swing from morning to afternoon is not something you see often at all! A fairly benign period followed through the 20th, with the only notable occurrence being a widespread rain event on Sunday the 15th, when several stations recorded an inch or more of rain as a moist low pressure system cut northeastward through the region. This was the heaviest rain of the month at all stations, with a lighter event on the 26th delivering generally a half inch or less to most. Before that event, we had a fairly heavy northwest flow snow from the 22nd to the 24th that produced multiple reports of 9” or greater at high elevations, with double digit totals at the ski resorts, Roan Mountain, and Wolf Laurel. High temperatures in the Foothills failed to escape the 30s on the 23rd, and flurries flew down to the South Carolina state line for much of the day. After that, the month went out quietly, with mildly above normal temperatures. Local Details Slightly Above Normal Temperatures

Precipitation was mostly below normal once again, marking the fourth straight month that much of the region experienced drier than average conditions. The Western Escarpment had better luck with a few northwest flow snow events, while the Foothills and SWNC stayed drier, as you can see from the map below. This all plays into the drought concerns as spring wears on. Apr 24, 2026 See all Golf-O-Meter 0 Golfballs Only ducks or polar bears. Good day for knitting. Maybe, if you are brave. Worth taking a chance. A good day to be outside. Bosses, you'd better stand at the door. (Or will bosses be first out the door?) CONTACT US Privacy Policy Terms and Conditions Contact Us
